cards.front: Zero Conditional — Structure?
cards.back:
If + Present Simple, Present Simple.
Used for: general truths, facts, natural laws.
Example: If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.

cards.front: Conditional I — Structure?
cards.back:
If + Present Simple, will + Infinitive.
Used for: probable future situations.
Example: If it rains, I will take an umbrella.

cards.front: Conditional II — Structure?
cards.back:
If + Past Simple, would + Infinitive.
Used for: hypothetical/unlikely present situations.
Example: If I had more time, I would learn Japanese.

cards.front: Conditional III — Structure?
cards.back:
If + Past Perfect, would have + Past Participle.
Used for: unreal past situations (can't be changed).
Example: If I had studied harder, I would have passed.

cards.front: Conditional II — Why 'were' instead of 'was'?
cards.back:
In formal English, 'were' is used for ALL persons in Conditional II:
If I were you, I would apologise.
If she were here, she would help.

cards.front: Conditional III — What variants can replace 'would have' in the main clause?
cards.back:
could have + PP (possibility)
might have + PP (weak possibility)
should have + PP (advice/regret)
Example: If I had known, I could have helped.

cards.front: Common mistake: 'If I would have known...' — What's wrong?
cards.back:
NEVER use 'would' in the if-clause!
Correct: If I HAD known...
The if-clause always uses Past Perfect in Conditional III.

cards.front: Zero Conditional — Can 'if' be replaced?
cards.back:
Yes, by 'when' — because it describes things that ALWAYS happen.
When you heat water, it boils. = If you heat water, it boils.

cards.front: Conditional I — What can replace 'will' in the main clause?
cards.back:
can, may, might, should, or an imperative.
If you see Tom, tell him to call me. (imperative)
If it rains, we can stay inside. (can)

cards.front: 'should have done' — What does it express?
cards.back:
Regret + criticism: It was WRONG not to do it.
I should have studied more. = Ich hätte mehr lernen sollen (habe es aber nicht getan).

cards.front: 'shouldn't have done' — What does it express?
cards.back:
Regret + criticism: It was WRONG to do it.
She shouldn't have said that. = Sie hätte das nicht sagen sollen (hat es aber getan).

cards.front: 'could have done' — What does it express?
cards.back:
A missed opportunity: You HAD the chance but didn't use it.
I could have gone to the party. = Ich hätte gehen können (bin aber nicht gegangen).

cards.front: 'should have' vs. 'could have' — What's the difference?
cards.back:
should have = it was WRONG (advice/criticism)
could have = you had the POSSIBILITY (neutral or regret)
I should have told her. (it was wrong not to)
I could have told her. (I had the chance but didn't)

cards.front: Common writing mistake with 'should have' / 'could have'?
cards.back:
WRONG: should of, could of
CORRECT: should've / should have, could've / could have
The spoken contraction sounds like 'of' but it's always 'have'!

cards.front: Prepositions of time: When do you use 'at'?
cards.back:
For specific times and points:
at 5 o'clock, at noon, at midnight, at night, at the weekend (BE), at Christmas, at the moment

cards.front: Prepositions of time: When do you use 'on'?
cards.back:
For days and dates:
on Monday, on 25th March, on my birthday, on Christmas Day, on the weekend (AE)

cards.front: Prepositions of time: When do you use 'in'?
cards.back:
For months, years, seasons, parts of the day:
in March, in 2026, in summer, in the morning/afternoon/evening

cards.front: When do you use NO preposition with time expressions?
cards.back:
After this/last/next/every + time word:
next Monday (NOT: on next Monday)
Also: today, tomorrow, yesterday, tonight

cards.front: 'since' vs. 'for' — What's the difference?
cards.back:
since = point in time: since 2020, since Monday
for = duration: for two weeks, for ages
Both used with Present Perfect:
I've lived here since 2020 / for six years.

cards.front: 'by' vs. 'until' — What's the difference?
cards.back:
by = at the latest (deadline): Finish by Friday.
until = continuously up to: I'll wait until Friday.
by = Punkt | until = Dauer

cards.front: Verb + Preposition: listen ___, look ___, look ___
cards.back:
listen TO (zuhören)
look AT (anschauen)
look FOR (suchen)

cards.front: Verb + Preposition: wait ___, depend ___, believe ___
cards.back:
wait FOR (warten auf)
depend ON (abhängen von)
believe IN (glauben an)

cards.front: Verb + Preposition: apologise ___, complain ___, succeed ___
cards.back:
apologise FOR (sich entschuldigen für)
complain ABOUT (sich beschweren über)
succeed IN (Erfolg haben bei)

cards.front: Adjective + Preposition: good ___, interested ___, afraid ___
cards.back:
good AT (gut in)
interested IN (interessiert an)
afraid OF (Angst haben vor)

cards.front: Adjective + Preposition: proud ___, angry ___ (person/thing)
cards.back:
proud OF (stolz auf)
angry WITH a person (wütend auf jemanden)
angry ABOUT a thing (wütend über etwas)

cards.front: Adjective + Preposition: different ___, famous ___, worried ___
cards.back:
different FROM (anders als)
famous FOR (berühmt für)
worried ABOUT (besorgt über)

cards.front: Noun + Preposition: reason ___, solution ___, effect ___
cards.back:
reason FOR (Grund für)
solution TO (Lösung für)
effect ON (Wirkung auf)

cards.front: Complete the Conditional III: 'If she ___ (not/eat) so much, she ___ (not/feel) sick.'
cards.back: If she HADN'T EATEN so much, she WOULDN'T HAVE FELT sick.

cards.front: Express regret: You forgot your best friend's birthday.
cards.back:
I should have remembered his/her birthday.
I could have at least sent a message.
I shouldn't have forgotten.

cards.front: Fill in: 'The meeting is ___ Monday ___ 3 pm ___ the afternoon.'
cards.back: The meeting is ON Monday AT 3 pm IN the afternoon.
